justification: All pass criteria met: Regional isolation model with independent EU/US/APAC cells where no raw PII crosses regions. GDPR and CCPA data residency explicitly addressed (encrypted cross-region replication rejected as still violating residency). Global dashboard uses push-based anonymized metrics aggregation — never queries regional databases directly. Courier-region binding enforced at domain level (CourierRegionBinding aggregate) and naturally by regional isolation. Trade-offs explicitly call out 3-region operational overhead (CI/CD complexity, infrastructure-as-code, centralized logging). Degraded EU service designed with circuit breaker, cache fallback, read-only mode, and explicit rejection of write operations during outage.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough regional isolation model — cell-based architecture with explicit data-residency-guard middleware as an architectural fitness function
Global dashboard design is clean: push-based anonymized metrics, no raw data access, with clear 5-10s staleness acceptance
Correctly rejected cross-region replication even with encryption, noting regulators consider encrypted PII in foreign jurisdictions a violation
JWT-based region routing over geo-IP is a smart design choice that eliminates VPN/proxy misrouting risks
Degraded mode is well-specified: read-only from cache, explicit timestamp labeling, write rejection with clear errors
Risks section correctly identifies operational complexity of 3 regional deployments as high severity with concrete mitigations
Implementation guidance includes a phased Strangler Fig migration approach, which is pragmatic for an existing EU deployment
APAC data residency requirements flagged as low-confidence but not explored — China's PIPL or Japan's APPI could impose materially different constraints than GDPR/CCPA
Schema migration risk across 3 regions mentioned in rubric expected risks but not explicitly called out as a risk — closest is the CI/CD mitigation under operational complexity

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Regional isolation model with independent EU/US/APAC data planes where no raw PII crosses regions. GDPR and CCPA data residency explicitly addressed with physical data separation by construction. Global dashboard uses pre-aggregated anonymized metrics only — no cross-region raw data access. Courier-region affinity enforced as a domain invariant via synchronous check at assignment time. Trade-offs extensively cover 3-region operational overhead (infrastructure-as-code, platform team, drift detection, CI/CD consistency). Degraded EU service designed with multi-AZ resilience, cached order status, static fallback, and explicit acknowledgment that cross-region failover is prohibited for PII.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough regional isolation design — PII isolation is enforced architecturally (no cross-region data path exists) rather than by policy, which is the strongest possible compliance posture
Global dashboard design is well-reasoned: pre-aggregated metrics with an explicit allowlist preventing PII leakage, staleness indicators, and fire-and-forget coupling so regions operate independently

Courier-region affinity modeled as a synchronous domain invariant with explicit rejection of cross-region assignments — correctly identifies this as a compliance-critical constraint, not just a business preference
Trade-offs are concrete and well-structured — each includes gains, sacrifices, and rejected alternatives with clear reasoning for rejection
Addresses the traveling customer edge case (US customer in EU routed to US data plane) with the correct compliance-over-latency tradeoff
Risks section correctly identifies operational complexity of 3 stacks as high severity and provides actionable mitigation (IaC, single pipeline, drift detection, platform team)
Schema migration risk across regions captured implicitly via the drift detection and single CI/CD pipeline guidance
APAC regulatory requirements (PDPA, PIPL) acknowledged as a low-confidence area but not analyzed — acceptable given prompt constraints but a gap for a production design
Phased implementation approach (EU refactor → US greenfield → APAC + dashboard) is pragmatic and reduces rollout risk

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Regional isolation model with fully autonomous cells per region (no raw data crosses regions). GDPR addressed via account-residency routing and EU cell isolation; CCAA addressed via US cell isolation. Global dashboard consumes only pre-aggregated anonymized metrics from the Global Control Plane, never cross-region raw data. Courier assignment is region-locked with immutable regionId enforced at the domain model level. Trade-offs explicitly cover 3-region operational overhead (Terraform templating, deployment consistency, per-region tooling). Degraded EU service designed with edge cache for stale reads and write queue for deferred mutations. No fail criteria triggered.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough cell-based architecture with defense-in-depth for data sovereignty: network-level egress controls, schema allowlist on metrics pipeline, and application-layer enforcement
Account-residency routing over IP geolocation is correctly identified as non-negotiable for GDPR compliance, with the traveling customer scenario explicitly addressed
Global dashboard design is well-articulated: push-based metrics aggregation with PII stripping, no inbound connections to regional cells, and clear separation of concerns via CQRS
Courier regionId immutability enforced as a domain invariant rather than a business logic check — architecturally impossible to violate, not just prohibited
Risks section identifies PII leakage through the metrics pipeline as critical severity with a concrete mitigation (allowlist, not blocklist)
Phased implementation guidance is pragmatic — hardening the EU cell first validates the architecture before adding multi-region complexity
APAC data residency requirements (PDPA, PIPL) are acknowledged as a low-confidence area but not concretely addressed — could have at least outlined how cell design might differ
Schema migration coordination across 3 regions (a key risk from the rubric) is not explicitly called out as a risk, though deployment consistency is mentioned in trade-offs
